summary: A 'How to Choose a Welder' selection chart that guides users through six questions—skill level, shielding gas need, material type, material thickness, typical applications, and required cleanliness—to recommend one of four welding processes: Flux-Cored/FCAW, MIG/GMAW, Stick/SMAW, or TIG/GTAW. A side panel addresses input voltage (120V or 240V) and MIG vs Flux-Cored selection, plus an explanation of duty cycle with an example (165A at 20% duty cycle = 2 minutes welding / 8 minutes resting in a 10-minute period).
section_hint: Welder Selection Chart / Process Selection Guide

figures.caption: How to Choose a Welder matrix comparing Flux-Cored/FCAW, MIG/GMAW, Stick/SMAW, and TIG/GTAW across skill level, shielding gas, material, thickness, applications, and cleanliness
Input voltage and MIG vs Flux-Cored selection guide indicating 120V vs 240V compatibility for each process
Duty Cycle example illustration: 165A at 20% duty cycle equals 2 minutes welding and 8 minutes resting per 10-minute period

tables.title: How to Choose a Welder
rows: Steel, Stainless Steel, Aluminum (spool gun required)
Steel, Stainless Steel, Chrome-Moly, Magnesium Alloys (AC required)
Galvanized steel; general fabrication; pipe and tubing; maintenance & repair
Sheet metal; autobody; tubing; structural steel
Pipe and tubing; structural steel; pressure vessels; maintenance & repair
Stainless steel exhausts; bicycle frames; thin wall pipe & tubing; metal art
Ideal for outdoor/windy conditions; forgiving on rusty/dirty steels; good out-of-position; high deposition rates
Fast production (high welding speeds); easiest to learn; clean welds with no slag; better control on thin materials
Ideal for outdoor/windy conditions; forgiving on rusty/dirty steels; deep penetration; good for thicker materials
Highest quality welds; extremely aesthetic welds; can be used on a variety of materials; precise control
